title: W2 D Suggested Exercises
body:
Suggested Exercises for Week 2 Juan Carlos Carbajal

ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026 UNSW Sydney

Problem 1. Let x < z < y be three real numbers. Show that z can be written as a convex combination of x and y. That is, find α ∈ (0, 1) such that z = αx + (1 − α)y. y−z Hint: Try α = y− x and then obtain 1 − α to form a convex combination of x and y.

Problem 2. We define a line in R2 as the set of vectors

L = {( x1 , x2 ) ∈ R2 : a1 x1 + a2 x2 = b},

for some fixed numbers a1 , a2 and b. Prove that L is a convex set. Notice the immediate economic implication: a budget line is a convex set.

Problem 3. Given a vector a ∈ R n , a ∕= 0, a hyperplane H through the origin is defined as the set of vectors H = { x ∈ R n : a · x = 0} .

The vector a is called the normal of the hyperplane H, and this last is said to pass through the origin (of the n-dimensional Euclidean space) because 0 ∈ H. The positive half-space H + associated to H is the set H + = { x ∈ R n : a · x ≥ 0} .

With the obvious modification one can define the negative half-space H − .

(a) Show that the half-space H + is convex (and of course so is H − ). (b) Show that H = H + ∩ H − . (c) Show that H is a convex set.

Problem 4. Recall that a set S ⊆ R n is convex if for any x, y ∈ S and any α ∈ [0, 1], one has that α x + (1 − α) y belongs to S. Suppose we take a convex combination of not just two but, let’s say K elements in S. That is, let x1 , x2 , . . . , xK be elements in S and let α1 , α2 , . . . , αK be such that 0 ≤ αk ≤ 1 for all k = 1, 2, . . . , K and ∑ αk = 1. Now consider the convex combination

K x = ∑ αk xk . k =1

When can we conclude that x ∈ S? The answer is provided in the following proposition, the proof of which is what this problem is about.

Proposition 1

The set S ⊆ R n is convex if, and only if, for any positive integer K, any x1 , x2 , . . . , xK with xk ∈ S for all k = 1, . . . , K, and any α1 , α2 , . . . , αK be such that 0 ≤ αk ≤ 1 for all k = 1, 2, . . . , K and ∑ αk = 1, the convex combination ∑ αk xk belongs to S.

Problem 5. Show that given the convex sets S1 , S2 , . . . , SK , the set

K 󰁟 S = Sk k =1

is also convex. Hint: Use an induction argument. We know that the statement is true for K = 2 (we have seen this in class). Assume that the statement is also true for arbitrary K − 1. Now show that it is true for K.

Problem 6. This problem asks you to establish general properties of Euclidean spaces. As an aside, these properties hold for any normed vector space, but we can restrict ourselves to R n .

Proposition 2 √ Let R n be the n-dimensional Euclidean space. Define the norm 󰀂 x󰀂 = x · x ≥ 0 induced by the inner product. Note that we can also understand the norm of x as its distance to 0. The following holds, for any x, y, z ∈ R n :

(a) z · z ≥ 0;

(b) x · y ≤ 󰀂 x󰀂 󰀂y󰀂;

(c) | x · y| ≤ 󰀂 x󰀂 󰀂y󰀂;

(d) 󰀂 x + y󰀂 ≤ 󰀂 x󰀂 + 󰀂y󰀂;

(e) | 󰀂 x󰀂 − 󰀂y󰀂 | ≤ 󰀂 x − y󰀂.

Parts (b) and (c) of the above proposition are known as the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (sometimes the CBS inequality, where the B stands for the Russian mathematician Bunyakovsky). Part (d) is obviously the triangle inequality. Hint: Part (a) is obvious from the definition of the inner product. The key for part (b) is to fix x, y ∈ R n and define z = αx − βy for any α, β ∈ R. Then use part (a) to obtain

0 ≤ α2 󰀂 x󰀂2 − 2αβ x · y + β2 󰀂y󰀂2 .

Finally, let α = 󰀂y󰀂 and β = 󰀂 x󰀂. Parts (c), (d) and (e) should follow easily from here.

Problem 7. Let x ∈ R n and consider the open ball B󰂃 ( x) with radius 󰂃 > 0. Show that B󰂃 ( x) is an open subset of R n .

Problem 8. Let R and consider the standard Euclidean metric on R, i.e., d E ( x, y) = | x − y| for all x, y ∈ R. For each of the following, state whether the set is open, closed, or compact (it might be more than one, or none of these). Explain your answer as clearly as possible. (a) A = {23, 25}. (b) B = (0, 1) ∪ [3, 4]; 󰀋 󰀌 (c) C = 1, 12 , 13 , 14 , . . . ∪ {0}; 󰀋 󰀌 (d) D = 1, 12 , 13 , 14 , . . . ; (e) E = {2, 4, 6, 8, . . .}, the set of even numbers.
